paragraphs.text: S
CONFEDERATION OF INDUSTRY OF THE CZECH REPUBLIC
SP
CONFEDERATION OF INDUSTRY OF THE CZECH REPUBLIC
PRAGUE, OCTOBER 22TH, 2025

The SPCR supports the European Commission’s goal of protecting consumers in the digital environment and strengthening trust in new technologies. However, the current EU regulatory framework already provides extensive consumer protection, including through the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), and the forthcoming AI Act.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: Therefore, it is essential that the DFA does not duplicate or extend existing obligations but instead focuses on effective and consistent enforcement of current rules and on targeted interventions only where clear regulatory gaps have been identified.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: The EU already has sufficient tools to address manipulative and deceptive online practices. The main issue lies in inconsistent and insufficient enforcement across Member States. Instead of introducing another layer of regulation, the Commission should strengthen coordination between enforcement authorities, for example, by revising the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Regulation.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Key Recommendations:
Priorities: simplification and enforcement of new regulations.

evidence_quotes.quote: introducing blanket prohibitions that could capture legitimate or innovative user interface designs.

paragraphs.text: These matters are already covered under the DSA, AI Act, and UCPD. Any new initiative should take a risk-based and technology-neutral approach rather than introducing blanket prohibitions that could capture legitimate or innovative user interface designs. The focus should be on clear definitions and practical guidance instead of broad restrictions.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Key Recommendations:
Ensure a coherent approach to “dark patterns” and “addictive design.”

paragraphs.text: Existing frameworks (e.g., UCPD, CRD, national consumer protection laws) already cover issues such as gamification, personalization, or pricing practices. Strengthening enforcement mechanisms would be more effective than creating overlapping rules, thereby ensuring legal certainty for consumers and businesses alike.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Key Recommendations:
Promote coordinated enforcement rather than expanding sectoral rules.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Key Recommendations:
Maintain proportionality and realistic implementation.

paragraphs.text: Additional or unclear compliance obligations would place a disproportionate burden on small and medium-sized enterprises. The DFA should follow the principle of proportionality, minimize administrative costs, and support an innovation-friendly and fair digital environment in the EU.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Key Recommendations:
Maintain proportionality and realistic implementation.

paragraphs.text: Consumer rules should remain clear, consistent, and predictable across the entire digital ecosystem. In this regard, we welcome the Commission’s Digital Simplification Omnibus initiative and recommend that any future DFA be closely aligned with it to ensure coherence and avoid regulatory overlap.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Adopt a horizontal and coherent approach to consumer protection.

paragraphs.text: The DFA should serve as a tool for simplification and coherence, not another layer of regulation that increases complexity and legal uncertainty. The priority must be coordination, consistent interpretation, and enforcement of existing rules. Only then can the EU strengthen consumer trust while ensuring that Europe remains an attractive environment for innovation and digital business.

section_path: Input by the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic (SPCR) – Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Conclusion

paragraphs.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
